title: Chapter 4: The Pink Windmill of the Vain Sorcerers
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D7+D8 -->
The Strawberry Dream Farm appeared on the horizon at 3:47 PM — a giant pink windmill rising from strawberry fields like a monument to everything wrong with modern America.
Chloe leaned forward, grip tightening on her sword. "The Windmill of Eternal Vanity."
"It's a strawberry farm," Sam said.
"No. The pink — not paint. Concentrated vanity energy, harvested from the self-obsessed. Every photo feeds it. Every hashtag makes it stronger."
"#StrawberryDreamFarm has 2.3 million posts."
"2.3 million souls."
Sam took a four-second sip of americano. "We're not attacking a strawberry farm."
"We're liberating it."
"It's a strawberry farm."
"It's a soul-harvesting engine of late-stage capitalism disguised as a strawberry farm. Same thing."
Sam pulled into the parking lot anyway, because she had to pee and the next rest stop was forty miles. "Fine. You can 'liberate' while I use the bathroom. Do not get arrested."
"The Queen does not get arrested. The Queen arrests others."
"You don't have arresting authority."
"I have a sword."
"It's plastic."
"The sword of truth transcends material limitations."

Sam walked into the gift shop. Through the window, she could see Chloe striding toward the windmill, poncho billowing, crown glinting. A group of influencers near the strawberry-shaped photo booth turned to stare.
"Oh no," Sam whispered. Then she ordered a large iced coffee. She was going to need it.
Outside, Chloe planted her foam sword in the dirt. "HALT!"
The influencers — four of them in beige — turned. One stopped mid-candid-laugh. Another lowered her strawberry.
"Who are you?" asked Bucket Hat Guy.
"Queen Artemis of Avalon. And you are draining souls into this pink abomination."
Bucket Hat Guy blinked. "It's a windmill."
"It's a soul engine. Every photo you take feeds it."
Candid Laugh Girl actually laughed — a real one this time. "Oh my god, is this performance art? Are you on TikTok?"
"I am on a quest," Chloe said. "Step away from the windmill or face the consequences."
"What consequences?"
Chloe raised her sword. "I'll explain your insecurities back to you in excruciating detail until you leave voluntarily."
Silence. Then Bucket Hat Guy: "...what?"

"You," Chloe said, pointing her sword at him. "You bought that bucket hat because an influencer you follow wore one and got 200K likes. You don't actually like bucket hats. You're wearing a forty-dollar insecurity on your head."
His hand drifted toward his hat, then stopped.

"And you," she continued, turning to Candid Laugh Girl. "You've been here for two hours taking photos trying to capture 'joy.' But you can't capture joy because you're not feeling joy. You're feeling pressure to perform joy for people you don't know."
Candid Laugh Girl's smile faltered.
"The strawberry you're photographing is just a strawberry," Chloe told Strawberry Girl. "It can't give you the validation you want. No fruit can. You need to call your mother."

Strawberry Girl looked at her phone. Then at the strawberry. Then at Chloe. "My mom has been texting me all week," she said quietly. "I've been ignoring her because I was busy... doing this." She gestured at the strawberry in her hand like it had suddenly turned into evidence.
"Call her," Chloe said. "Grass is free. Self-reflection is harder."
Strawberry Girl put down the strawberry. She walked toward the parking lot, phone already at her ear.
"Did you just... convert someone?" Bucket Hat Guy asked.
"Exorcised," Chloe corrected. "Lesser vanity demon. Common in this area."

Candid Laugh Girl had stopped laughing. She sat down on the edge of the photo booth platform, scrolling through her own Instagram feed. The expression on her face was the expression of someone who had just realized they'd spent three hours trying to look happy and hadn't actually felt happy for any of them.
"She's kind of right," Candid Laugh Girl said. "This is... exhausting."
"The windmill feeds on exhaustion," Chloe said. "The more tired you are, the harder you try to prove you're not tired. It's a closed loop. The only way out is to stop."
Bucket Hat Guy took off his bucket hat. He looked at it. "It was forty-two dollars."
"The first step is admitting you have a problem," Chloe said.

Sam came out of the gift shop holding two iced coffees. She surveyed the scene: Candid Laugh Girl sitting on the photo booth, looking at her phone with the expression of a hostage realizing the ransom was her own self-worth. Bucket Hat Guy holding his hat like it was a dead pet. Strawberry Girl nowhere to be seen — presumably calling her mother. And Chloe, standing in front of the pink windmill with her foam sword raised, the setting sun making her plastic crown actually look kind of majestic.
"What did I miss?" Sam asked, handing Chloe a coffee.
"I liberated three souls from the Vanity Engine."
"You bullied three influencers into having existential crises."
"Same thing."
Sam took a sip of her coffee. "I got it on video."
Chloe's eyes lit up. "You did?"
"Someone's going to post it and it's going to get two million views and you're going to be internet famous whether you want to be or not."
Chloe considered this. "I'll accept my role as folk hero with humility."
"You don't have humility."
"I have the appearance of humility, which is the same thing in the digital age."
Sam couldn't argue with that.
They drove away at sunset. Behind them, the pink windmill kept spinning — but three fewer people were standing in front of it, and one of them was on the phone with her mother.
"That's one," Chloe said, watching the windmill shrink in the rearview mirror. "One engine down."
"It's a strawberry farm."
"It's a strawberry farm with three fewer souls trapped inside it."
Sam opened her mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. "Fine. That counts."
Track eight on the playlist: Fleetwood Mac. Chloe turned it up.
*And the landslide brought me down...*
"Where to next?" Sam asked.
"South," Chloe said. "The Kingdom of Avalon awaits."
"That's just Los Angeles."
"Avalon."
"They have a Six Flags."
"Avalon."
Sam sighed. She didn't change the destination on her GPS. But she also didn't correct Chloe again.
Chloe stared out the window, then suddenly turned to face the empty back seat.
"That was a good chapter," she said to no one.
Sam glanced over. "Who are you talking to?"
"The readers. That windmill scene had solid pacing. Good banter. The emotional arc with Strawberry Girl landed."
"There's no one in the back seat."
"There are thousands of people in the back seat. They're just in a different dimension."
Sam looked in the rearview mirror. The back seat was empty. She looked anyway.
"Can they hear me?" Sam asked.
"Yes."

Sam thought about this. Then she adjusted the rearview mirror down so it pointed at the empty seat and said: "She's like this all the time. It doesn't get better. But somehow it also doesn't get worse. Send help."
Chloe laughed. A real laugh — the first one Sam had heard since the Zoom meeting.
"That's going in the book," Chloe said.
"What book?"
"This book. The one we're in right now."
Sam took a very long sip of her iced coffee.
